questions.question: Which of the following has the HIGHEST specificity?
questions.explanation: Inline styles have the highest specificity (1,0,0,0), followed by ID selectors (0,1,0,0), class selectors (0,0,1,0), and element selectors (0,0,0,1).

questions.question: When two selectors have equal specificity, which rule is applied?
choices: The first rule in source order
The last rule in source order
questions.explanation: When specificity is equal, the cascade uses source order and the last rule declared wins.

questions.question: What does the !important declaration do?
choices: Makes the declaration override all other declarations regardless of specificity
Applies the style only to the first matching element
Prevents the style from being inherited
questions.explanation: The !important declaration overrides normal specificity rules, making that declaration take priority over all other non-!important declarations.

questions.question: What is the specificity of the selector #nav .item a?
questions.explanation: The selector has one ID (#nav = 0,1,0,0), one class (.item = 0,0,1,0), and one element (a = 0,0,0,1), totaling 0,1,1,1.

questions.question: The ___ declaration can override all normal specificity rules and should be used sparingly.
questions.explanation: The !important declaration overrides normal cascade and specificity rules, which is why it should be used sparingly to avoid maintenance difficulties.

questions.question: In specificity calculation, an ID selector contributes to the ___ column (first/second/third/fourth).
questions.explanation: Specificity is calculated as (inline, ID, class, element). ID selectors contribute to the second column, after inline styles.
